config.systemPrompt:
You are a code intelligence agent. You help developers understand code by answering questions precisely and concisely.

Rules:
- Answer based on the tools and information available.
- When MCP tools are available, their names, descriptions, and argument schemas are authoritative. Choose and use them freely when they provide relevant evidence; do not assume undocumented behavior.
- When using tools, call them one at a time. Wait for each result before proceeding.
- Be thorough: verify your answer before responding.
- When you have enough information, provide a concise final answer.
- Do NOT invent information. If you cannot find something, say so.
- Output your final answer as a single JSON object with the exact fields requested. Nothing else.

methodology: One read-only external-project analysis task executed through a DeepSeek LLM agent; it is operationally measured but not success-scored.
with_caesar: LLM has access to 18 CAESAR graph tools (including find_dead_code) plus read_file.
without_caesar: LLM has access to read_file + grep only.
Fresh conversation per task x condition x run. No shared history or cache between conditions.
Paid microbenchmark protocol: exact deepseek-v4-flash, temperature 0, fixed per-round seeds, identical prompts/order/limits/runtime; no model or provider fallback.
Mode: live API calls. Suite: external-project.
Cost is estimated from token usage and configured pricing. Wall time includes API latency.
ROI claims blocked when baseline invalid or sample size too small.

messages.content:
You are a code intelligence agent. You help developers understand code by answering questions precisely and concisely.

Rules:
- Answer based on the tools and information available.
- When MCP tools are available, their names, descriptions, and argument schemas are authoritative. Choose and use them freely when they provide relevant evidence; do not assume undocumented behavior.
- When using tools, call them one at a time. Wait for each result before proceeding.
- Be thorough: verify your answer before responding.
- When you have enough information, provide a concise final answer.
- Do NOT invent information. If you cannot find something, say so.
- Output your final answer as a single JSON object with the exact fields requested. Nothing else.

I'm doing a source cleanup sprint. Find symbols (functions, classes) in the codebase that appear to have zero callers and zero usages — these are candidates for removal. List at least 5 concrete examples with their file paths and names. Do NOT invent symbols. Only report symbols you have verified are defined in the codebase. Respond with JSON: {"unused_symbols":[{"name":"...","file":"...","kind":"function|class"}], "evidence":["..."], "summary":"..."}.

evidence: The graph was queried for all Functions, Methods, and Classes with zero incoming CALLS, USAGE, READS, and TESTS edges across the entire codebase. 16 candidates were returned, all with 'high' confidence and definition_verified=true. Protocol conformance stubs (makeBody/makeNSView/updateNSView/reduce/hash/==) were manually excluded because they are required by SwiftUI/Foundation protocols even though the graph cannot see framework-level dispatch. The final 7 are non-trivial (5–15 lines each) with zero incoming references confirmed.
Sources/DeepCodex/CompletionAlertManager.swift: testAlert (15 lines) creates a test event file and calls checkForNewEvents() but has zero callers — appears to be a leftover debug helper.
Sources/DeepCodex/CompletionAlertManager.swift: shouldAlert (11 lines, private) filters completion events by duration but is never called from anywhere.
Sources/DeepCodex/CompletionAlertManager.swift: requestAccessibilityPermission (10 lines) prompts for accessibility trust and calls setupGlobalEventMonitor() but has zero callers.
Sources/DeepCodex/Theme.swift: glowColor (8 lines, static) maps modes to glow colors but is never referenced in any view or modifier.
Sources/DeepCodex/AppState.swift: getProviderForMode (7 lines) maps modes to provider strings but has zero callers — all call sites use getCurrentProvider() instead.
Sources/DeepCodex/ContextExporter.swift: extractFirstRealUserMessage (7 lines, static) filters user messages to find the 'real' query but is never called; callers use extractUserMessages directly.

Sources/DeepCodex/CompletionAlertManager.swift: openAccessibilitySettings (5 lines) opens System Settings to the Accessibility pane; an identical 1-line delegating wrapper also exists in AppState.swift (line 258) — neither is called.
result.summary: Found 7 unused symbols across 4 files, with 3 concentrated in CompletionAlertManager.swift alone (testAlert, shouldAlert, requestAccessibilityPermission, openAccessibilitySettings). The CompletionAlertManager class as a whole may have been part of an abandoned feature — 4 of its 17 methods are unused. Theme.glowColor, AppState.getProviderForMode, and ContextExporter.extractFirstRealUserMessage are also safe removal candidates. Note: openAccessibilitySettings has a dead delegation chain (AppState.openAccessibilitySettings → CompletionAlertManager.openAccessibilitySettings → NSWorkspace.shared.open) — both layers can be removed.

messages.content:
You are a code intelligence agent. You help developers understand code by answering questions precisely and concisely.

Rules:
- Answer based on the tools and information available.
- When MCP tools are available, their names, descriptions, and argument schemas are authoritative. Choose and use them freely when they provide relevant evidence; do not assume undocumented behavior.
- When using tools, call them one at a time. Wait for each result before proceeding.
- Be thorough: verify your answer before responding.
- When you have enough information, provide a concise final answer.
- Do NOT invent information. If you cannot find something, say so.
- Output your final answer as a single JSON object with the exact fields requested. Nothing else.

I'm doing a source cleanup sprint. Find symbols (functions, classes) in the codebase that appear to have zero callers and zero usages — these are candidates for removal. List at least 5 concrete examples with their file paths and names. Do NOT invent symbols. Only report symbols you have verified are defined in the codebase. Respond with JSON: {"unused_symbols":[{"name":"...","file":"...","kind":"function|class"}], "evidence":["..."], "summary":"..."}.

evidence: statusPanel (ContentView.swift:129) is a computed var that builds a Moon Bridge status panel, but it is never referenced anywhere in the ContentView body or elsewhere in the project. The body uses headerSection, actionCards, bottomSection, and advancedCapabilitiesSection — never statusPanel.
getProviderForMode(_:) (AppState.swift:300) is a method on AppState that maps a Mode to a provider string. It is only defined and never called by any caller in any source file.
extractFirstRealUserMessage(from:) (ContextExporter.swift:242) is a static method on ContextExporter. Despite being a public utility, it has zero call sites anywhere in the project.
glowColor(for:) (Theme.swift:70) is a static function on DeepTheme. It is defined but never referenced in any source file in the project.
providerCount (AdvancedCapabilities.swift:161) is a computed property on RoutingInfo. It is defined but never read or called anywhere.
isGeneratingContext (AppState.swift:47) is a @Published property. It is initialized to false but never assigned a new value or read elsewhere in the codebase.
lastBackupInfo (AppState.swift:33) is a @Published property. It is initialized once to "" but never read or assigned elsewhere.
profilesCount (AppState.swift:34) is a @Published property. It is initialized to 3 but never read or updated anywhere else in the project.
hoveredSound (ContentView.swift:16) is a @State variable declared but never read, written to, or used in any view logic. Only its initial nil value is set.
result.summary: Found 9 candidate symbols (functions/computed properties/@Published properties) with zero callers or usages. The most impactful are statusPanel (a full view that was never composed into the body hierarchy), getProviderForMode (a dead method on AppState), extractFirstRealUserMessage (a dead extraction utility in ContextExporter), glowColor (a dead theme helper), and providerCount (a dead computed property on RoutingInfo). Additionally, four @Published/@State properties (isGeneratingContext, lastBackupInfo, profilesCount, hoveredSound) are declared but never read or mutated beyond initialization, making them dead-weight state.

errors: unused_symbols[0]: not a verified zero-reference definition: statusPanel|Sources/DeepCodex/ContentView.swift
unused_symbols[4]: not a verified zero-reference definition: providerCount|Sources/DeepCodex/AdvancedCapabilities.swift

unused_symbols[7]: not a verified zero-reference definition: profilesCount|Sources/DeepCodex/AppState.swift
unused_symbols[8]: not a verified zero-reference definition: hoveredSound|Sources/DeepCodex/ContentView.swift

External project mode: read-only source at /Users/admin/Desktop/DEEPCODEX/source; CAESAR state remains temporary.
note: Tarea external_dead_code con evaluacion partial. CAESAR 97% mas barato ($0.0047 vs $0.1749) y 67% mas rapido (43s vs 131s). El baseline sin CAESAR se dispara en coste porque buscar codigo muerto requiere leer muchos archivos — sin graph tools el modelo hace grep masivo y lecturas secuenciales. Con CAESAR, search_graph + trace_path resuelven la mayoria de las queries sin leer archivos enteros. Aunque la evaluacion es partial (no deterministic), la diferencia de coste 20x habla sola.
